metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Creative Writing 10 introduces students to creative writing as a craft and a practice. Students read like writers — noticing how authors make decisions about form, voice, and language — and then apply those observations in their own original writing. The course covers fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, and script, encouraging risk-taking, experimentation, and perseverance. Students learn that creative writers are attentive observers of the world.
metadata.general_description: A study of creative writing as a craft, developing students as writers of fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, and script through reading, experimentation, and a writer's workshop approach.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading widely across genres and cultures gives creative writers the raw material of human experience to draw from in their own work.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every creative work — including our own — is shaped by who we are, where we come from, and when we're writing. Creative writers are aware of these influences.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Creative writers make deliberate choices about words, images, structure, and form because they understand that language is a powerful tool for evoking emotion and shaping thought.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers take risks and persevere.
big_ideas.elaboration: Great writing requires the courage to experiment, fail, and try again. Writers grow through drafts, feedback, and the willingness to revise.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers are observant of the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Skilled writers pay close attention to people, places, language, and experience. Observation is the foundation of authentic writing.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives, values, beliefs, and points of view.
i_can_statements: I can read First Peoples creative works with attention to their cultural context and storytelling traditions.
I can identify how First Peoples oral narrative techniques (e.g., circular structure, repetition) can enrich my own creative writing.
I can engage with First Peoples texts using appropriate protocols and respect for cultural ownership.

competencies.raw: Access information for diverse purposes and from varied sources to inform writing; apply comprehension strategies to texts.
i_can_statements: I can read mentor texts closely to identify specific craft techniques I can try in my own writing.
I can research settings, periods, or experiences to write about them authentically.
I can apply reading strategies to understand complex or unfamiliar creative texts.

competencies.raw: Think critically, creatively, and reflectively to explore ideas within, between, and beyond texts; identify bias, contradictions, and distortions.
i_can_statements: I can analyze a creative text to understand how an author made specific craft decisions.
I can identify stereotypes or distortions in a creative work and consider how my own writing can avoid or subvert them.
I can reflect on how my reading is shaping my writing practice.

competencies.raw: Explore how language constructs personal and cultural identities; construct meaningful personal connections.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a creative writer expresses their cultural identity through their work.
I can make connections between my own life and experiences and the creative work I read.
I can explore my own identity through creative writing.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas and viewpoints from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding.
i_can_statements: I can participate in a writing workshop, sharing my work and responding constructively to others'.
I can give specific, kind, and helpful feedback on a peer's creative writing.
I can use workshop feedback to improve my own writing.

competencies.raw: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways; assess and refine texts through language manipulation.
i_can_statements: I can write a creative response to a text that uses its themes, images, or structure as a springboard.
I can experiment with language in revision — trying different words, images, or sentence structures to see what works best.
I can explain the craft decisions I made in a piece of writing and why I made them.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create engaging texts; express and support opinions with evidence.
i_can_statements: I can move through a full creative writing process: generating ideas, drafting, workshopping, revising, and finishing.
I can experiment with multiple approaches to the same piece before committing to one.
I can support creative choices in my writing with craft reasoning — explaining what I was trying to achieve.

competencies.raw: Use Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation conventions appropriately; use acknowledgements and citations; transform ideas to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can apply conventions of spelling, grammar, and punctuation in my creative writing.
I can acknowledge any sources or texts that inspired or influenced my work.
I can produce original creative writing in multiple forms (fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, script).

areas.name: Text Forms and Genres (Reading as a Writer)
areas.raw: Literary or thematic categories including adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, picture book, science fiction, biography, essay, journalism, manual, memoir, personal narrative, and speech.
topics: Reading as a writer: identifying craft techniques in published fiction, poetry, and non-fiction
Fiction genres: short story, flash fiction, novel excerpt, genre fiction
Poetry: lyric, narrative, spoken word, prose poetry, experimental
Creative non-fiction: personal essay, memoir, literary journalism
Script: stage play, screenplay, radio drama
First Peoples creative texts: oral tradition, contemporary Indigenous writing

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Narrative structures found in First Peoples texts (circular, iterative, cyclical); form, function, and genre; visual/graphic elements.
topics: Narrative structures: linear, circular, iterative, non-linear
First Peoples narrative structures: circular, iterative, seasonal/cyclical
Point of view: first, second, and third person (limited and omniscient)
Framing devices and narrative distance
Form and structure in poetry: stanza, line break, white space

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies; oral language strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes.
topics: Generating ideas: observation, freewriting, character development, 'what if' questions
Drafting: letting the first draft be messy and exploratory
Workshopping: structured peer feedback focused on craft
Revising: making substantive changes based on feedback and reflection
Editing: polishing grammar, syntax, and conventions
Metacognitive reflection: keeping a writer's notebook, tracking growth

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style (diction, vocabulary, sentence structure, tone); voice exploration (point of view, humour, irony, satire, wit, perspective, persona); usage and conventions; literary elements and devices.
topics: Diction: word choice and its effect on tone and meaning
Sentence variety: rhythm, length, and structure in creative writing
Developing a distinctive voice: tone, perspective, persona
Humour, irony, satire, and wit as creative modes
Figurative language: metaphor, simile, personification, imagery — used purposefully
Dialogue: crafting realistic and revealing character speech
Show, don't tell: using concrete detail instead of abstract description
Experimental language use: fragments, non-standard punctuation, invented words
